sentenceFrame: The goat is in the snow.
celebration: Two spellings for long o — oa (boat, goat) and ow (snow, grow). ow leads a quiet double life: here it says /oʊ/, but at the owl in Week 52 the same two letters will say /aʊ/. Acknowledge it, don't dwell. Right-branch leaf #3.

songs.title: Oh-Oh-Snow!
songs.lyrics:
[Intro]
(oh… oh… oh…)
Oh! Oh! Snow!

[Hook]
O-A in the middle! (boat! goat!)
O-W at the end! (snow! grow!)
Oh-oh-snow! Oh-oh-snow!
Two letters, one sound! (oh!)

[Verse 2]
B-O-A-T — boat! (on the boat!)
S-N-O-W — snow! (in the snow!)
Oh-oh-snow! Oh-oh-snow!
The O says its name! (oh!)

[Verse 3]
Row the boat! (row! row!)
The snow can grow! (grow! grow!)
Oh-oh-snow! Oh-oh-snow!
The goat is in the snow!

[Bridge]
A potato?! (NO!)
P-p-potato! In the snow!
(oh… oh… oh…)

[Outro]
Boat… goat… snow… (shhh…)
Oh! Oh! Snow!
songs.sunoStyle: dark trap, 68 bpm, heavy 808 bass, sparse hi-hats, deep whisper-rap verses, kids choir chant on hook, playful spooky, minimal, clean vocals, nursery trap
songs.sunoNotes: oa and ow both sing long-o /oʊ/ (phonetic respelling: sung 'oh', printed oa/ow). Positional lesson: 'O-A in the middle, O-W at the end.' Spell-outs uppercase. Potato in the snow gag. Do NOT sing the /aʊ/ 'ow' here — that's Week 52; keep every ow long and round.

songs.title: The Goat in the Snow
songs.lyrics:
[Intro]
(We can read. We can read!)

[Hook]
The goat is in the snow!
The goat is in the snow!
Snow, snow! (so much snow!)
The goat is in the snow!

[Verse 2]
The goat is on the boat!
Row, goat, row! (row! row!)
Snow, snow! (so much snow!)
The snow can grow!

[Verse 3]
Sheep is in the snow!
Bee is cold — brr! (so cold!)
Snow, snow! (so much snow!)
The goat can row the boat!

[Outro]
The goat… the snow… (shhh…)
In the snow?! (a potato!)
(POTATO!)
songs.sunoStyle: dark trap, 68 bpm, heavy 808 bass, sparse hi-hats, deep whisper-rap verses, kids choir chant on hook, playful spooky, minimal, clean vocals, nursery trap
songs.sunoNotes: Frame: 'The goat is in the snow.' — goat is a story friend (lowercase, fully decodable), not a named cast member. Sheep and Bee brave the cold. Whisper the outro, potato hits loud as a tiny snowman.

spreads.artNote: Fat soft snowflakes drifting through the spotlight, a white blanket beginning to gather. Winter arrives.
A sturdy little goat with a shaggy coat standing in the spotlight, breath steaming. A story friend — 'goat' is fully decodable now.

spreads.text: The goat is in the snow.
spreads.artNote: The goat trotting happily through deep snow, hoofprints behind it, snow on its nose.
A little rowboat pulled up at the edge of a frozen lake, oars ready.

spreads.text: The goat is on the boat.
spreads.artNote: The goat balancing bravely in the rowboat, hooves on the oars, ready to go.
The goat heaving on the oars, the boat gliding across dark water past drifting snow. Effort and joy.
A snowdrift piling higher and higher beside the goat, up to its shoulders now. The snow keeps growing.
Sheep — fluffy round white sheep with kind dark eyes — nearly camouflaged in the snow, only its dark eyes and pink nose showing. Bee shivers on its back.
A little snowman built around a single potato, with a carrot nose and stick arms. No noun printed — the class SHOUTS it. The goat peers over the drift.

sentences: The goat is in the snow.
The goat is on the boat.
extras: TWO SPELLINGS FOR LONG O: oa lives in the MIDDLE (boat, goat, coat, road); ow often lives at the END (snow, grow, show, row). Both say /oʊ/. Sort picture cards into two baskets — middle vs end.

THE ow WARNING (say it plainly): ow has TWO jobs. This week it says long o (snow). At Week 52 (owl, cow) the same letters will say /aʊ/. Tell the children now so it isn't a shock later — 'ow is a two-faced team.'
DO NOT introduce 'know' — its silent k waits for Week 56. If a child writes it, praise the try and park the word.
Right-branch leaf #3 goes up: read boat/goat/snow/grow aloud, hang the oa/ow leaf.
Moveable alphabet: build b-oa-t, g-oa-t, s-n-ow, g-r-ow — weld each vowel team.

teacherFocus: LONG O, TWO SPELLINGS. oa in the middle (boat, goat, coat, road); ow often at the end (snow, grow, show, row). Both say /oʊ/. Sort by position. Say the ow warning out loud: ow is a two-faced team — long o this week, but /aʊ/ at Week 52 (owl, cow). Naming it now prevents confusion later. Do NOT introduce 'know' — its silent k belongs to Week 56. Hang right-branch leaf #3.
